title: Reading and Use of English - Part 7
description: You are going to read an article about four people who describe learning a completely new skill as an adult. For questions 43–52, choose from the people (A–D). The people may be chosen more than once.

content.title: It's Never Too Late: Four People on Learning New Skills as Adults

texts.A:
### Vivienne, 40, Solicitor
I decided to learn the piano at forty, having done no musical training whatsoever since giving up the recorder at the age of nine. [43]My reason was quite specific: I had read that learning an instrument as an adult actively strengthens neural connections in a way that may help slow cognitive decline, and that struck me as an entirely sensible investment.[/43] I found a teacher who specialises in adult beginners, which turned out to be crucial – the approach is completely different from teaching children. [47]The most humbling part has been accepting that my progress will be slow and that there are no shortcuts – at work I'm used to being competent, and being a novice again was unexpectedly hard on my sense of self.[/47] [51]At my teacher's end-of-year recital last December I gave a short performance – just a two-minute piece – and the nervousness I felt was unlike anything I experience in a courtroom, which I found both amusing and genuinely illuminating.[/51]
texts.B:
### Raj, 51, Civil Engineer
[44]I took up watercolour painting on the advice of my therapist after a period of burnout – she suggested I needed a creative activity that had no correct answer and no deadline.[/44] As an engineer, the idea of doing something with no objectively right outcome was profoundly unfamiliar territory. [48]What I discovered almost immediately is that watercolour is extraordinarily difficult to control – the paint does what it wants, and the skill lies in learning to work with that rather than against it.[/48] This turned out to be a useful life lesson well beyond the canvas. [52]My wife initially worried I was simply finding a new way to be a perfectionist, but after six months she said she could see a genuine change in how I approach problems at work and at home.[/52]
texts.C:
### Tess, 32, Stay-at-home Parent
[45]I started learning to code when my youngest child began nursery and I found myself with three hours a day I didn't quite know what to do with.[/45] I had studied humanities at university and had always assumed computing was not for me, but online tutorials made the subject far more accessible than I'd ever expected. [49]What kept me going through the frustrating early stages was the very concrete satisfaction of making something work – seeing a page render or a function execute correctly feels like solving a puzzle, and nothing else I've done quite matches that feeling.[/49] I've now completed a professional web development certificate and I'm applying for junior developer roles, which still feels slightly surreal.
texts.D:
### Alun, 64, Retired GP
[46]I grew up in North Wales but never learned to speak Welsh properly, and when I retired I decided it was time to repair what I think of as a gap in my cultural identity.[/46] I enrolled in an intensive course and supplemented it with Welsh-language radio, podcasts and a conversation group that meets weekly at my local library. [50]What I hadn't appreciated was how learning Welsh would reconnect me with things I'd half-forgotten – local history, place names, a body of poetry – as though I were recovering something lost rather than simply acquiring something new.[/50] My grandchildren find it funny that I practise with them, but they've started picking up words themselves, which pleases me enormously.

questions.question: describes a specific, evidence-based reason for choosing the skill they decided to learn?
questions.explanation: Vivienne had read that learning an instrument as an adult strengthens neural connections and may slow cognitive decline, which she found a sensible reason to begin.

questions.question: was advised by a professional to take up a creative activity as part of their recovery?
questions.explanation: Raj's therapist suggested he needed a creative activity with no correct answer or deadline following a period of burnout.

questions.question: began learning their new skill when a change in their domestic circumstances gave them available time?
questions.explanation: Tess started learning to code when her youngest child started nursery and she found herself with three free hours a day.

questions.question: chose a skill that is connected to their cultural or personal heritage?
questions.explanation: Alun decided to learn Welsh properly in retirement, describing it as repairing a gap in his cultural identity.

questions.question: found the experience of being a complete beginner again unexpectedly challenging to their sense of self-worth?
questions.explanation: Vivienne says she is used to being competent at work and that being a novice again was unexpectedly hard on her sense of self.

questions.question: discovered that the skill they were learning required them to accept a loss of control?
questions.explanation: Raj found that watercolour paint does what it wants and the skill lies in working with it rather than against it.

questions.question: found that seeing a task work correctly provided the main motivation to keep learning?
questions.explanation: Tess says the concrete satisfaction of making something work – seeing a page render or a function execute – kept her going through the frustrating early stages.

questions.question: describes their new skill as reconnecting them with something from their past?
questions.explanation: Alun says learning Welsh reconnected him with history, place names and poetry he'd half-forgotten, as though recovering something lost rather than learning something new.

questions.question: performed their new skill in front of an audience for the first time and was surprised by how nervous they felt?
questions.explanation: Vivienne performed a two-minute piano piece at a recital and says the nervousness was unlike anything she experiences in a courtroom.

questions.question: mentions that someone close to them noticed a positive change in their behaviour as a result of the skill they learned?
questions.explanation: Raj's wife told him after six months that she could see a genuine change in how he approaches problems, despite her initial worry about his perfectionism.
